planting.note: Plant in frost-free, humid tropical conditions; pitomba is slow-growing but long-lived — choose a permanent position with well-drained but consistently moist soil.

directions: Plant in full sun in rich, moist but well-drained, slightly acidic soil in frost-free conditions; pitomba is a small to medium Brazilian Eugenia producing clusters of golden-orange round to ovoid fruits about cherry-sized with smooth thin skin and juicy sweet-acidic orange flesh surrounding a single large seed — the flavor blends apricot, mango, and peach with a pleasant tartness. Trees are slow to mature but once established bear heavy crops excellent eaten fresh, juiced, or preserved as jams. Slightly more cold-tolerant than many tropical Eugenias, tolerating brief dips near 30°F on established trees. A rewarding and unusual addition to the zone 10–12 food garden.

common_name: Hog Plum (Wild Olive, Seaside Plum, Tallow Wood)

planting.note: Plant in well-drained coastal or dry tropical soils; hog plum is extremely drought-tolerant once established and thrives in thin sandy or rocky soils where little else grows.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil — tolerates poor, rocky, and alkaline soils exceptionally well once established; hog plum is a pantropical shrub or small tree with strongly fragrant white flowers and small oval fruits that ripen from green to yellow-orange then red, with thin skin, juicy sour-sweet flesh, and a large oily seed. The fruit is eaten fresh when fully ripe but is very sour; it makes excellent jellies, juices, and fermented drinks popular across sub-Saharan Africa, the Caribbean, and coastal Brazil. The seeds contain a high-quality oil used for cooking and cosmetics in West Africa. A superb drought- and salt-spray-tolerant fruiting hedge plant for coastal gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit Factsheets

planting.note: Plant both male and female plants for reliable fruiting (plants are dioecious); some nurseries offer self-fertile selections that fruit without a pollinator.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile to average soil — tolerates drought, poor soils, and coastal conditions once established; governor's plum is a thorny tropical small tree or large shrub from Africa and Asia producing prolific clusters of small (1/2–1 inch) dark red to purple-black fruits with a flavor blending cherry and plum with pleasant tartness. One of the most reliable and productive tropical fruit plants for the subtropical home garden, fruiting heavily from a young age with minimal care beyond adequate drainage. The spiny branching habit makes it a formidable security hedge. Fruit is eaten fresh when fully ripe, made into jams, jellies, and juices, or fermented into alcoholic beverages in East Africa.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit Factsheets

planting.note: Plant in humid tropical lowlands with well-drained soil; requires separate male and female trees for fruiting unless self-fertile selected varieties are obtained.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, moist, well-drained, fertile soil in humid tropical lowland conditions; rukam is a large Southeast Asian fruiting tree in the same genus as governor's plum but bearing larger, rounder fruits (3/4–1 inch) with dark red to purple-black skin and acidic flesh that sweetens with full ripeness and can be eaten fresh or preserved. A handsome ornamental tree with large glossy leaves, it is grown in home gardens across Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines as both ornamental and fruit tree. Much less commonly grown than governor's plum but produces superior fruit quality and larger individual fruits. Slow to bear from seed — grafted plants fruit several years earlier.

planting.note: Plant bare-root in fall or early spring; seed requires 60–90 days cold-moist stratification for germination; plant male and female shrubs for reliable fruiting as plants are dioecious.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Indian plum is a Pacific Northwest native deciduous shrub prized for being one of the earliest-blooming woody plants — small clusters of fragrant white flowers appear in February or March before leaves emerge, often while snow is still on the ground, providing an invaluable nectar source for early bees and hummingbirds. Small oval drupes (like tiny olives) ripen to blue-black with a single large stone; the flavor is astringent and bitter raw but palatable to birds and was eaten sparingly by many Native peoples. An excellent choice for wildlife habitat gardens, riparian buffers, and native plant landscapes in the Pacific Northwest and northern California.
